serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kung Fu Chicken is approximately 200 g, representing one individual portion of the stir-fried chicken dish as served in a Singapore hawker centre or Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Kung Fu Chicken — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kung Fu Chicken (cr_id G00010375). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on stir-fried chicken with mixed sauce and cornstarch thickening
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kung Fu Chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous stir-fried chicken dishes with soy-based sauce and cornstarch thickening from the University of Sydney GI Database. Cornstarch contributes a moderate GI component but the overall dish GI is moderated by protein (9.76 g/100g), fat (6.98 g/100g), vinegar (acetic acid reduces glycaemic response), and relatively low carbohydrate (6.87 g/100g). GL per 200 g serving = (45 × 6.87 × 2) / 100 = 6.2 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 402 mg/100g with insufficient potassium (218 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g) to offset sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request low-sodium soy sauce preparation to reduce sodium intake; avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments or dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.87 g/100g), low sugar (3.03 g/100g), good protein (9.76 g/100g), and vinegar in sauce may attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kung Fu Chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 200 g serving; pair with non-starchy vegetables or brown rice rather than white rice to maintain glycaemic control, and request reduced cornstarch thickening if possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 402 mg/100g is elevated for cardiovascular health; cholesterol 26 mg/100g is moderate; saturated fat data unavailable but total fat 6.98 g/100g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kung Fu Chicken in portions of 150 g or less, request low-sodium soy sauce, and avoid additional salt or high-sodium side dishes to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits of 600 mg per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chili (nightshade) may trigger RA symptoms in sensitive individuals; sodium 402 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request chili-free preparation and reduced soy sauce to lower sodium below 400 mg/100g; ginger and garlic in the dish provide anti-inflammatory gingerols and allicin which are beneficial if nightshade and sodium concerns are addressed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kung Fu Chicken (功夫鸡) is a Chinese-Singaporean stir-fried chicken dish popular in Singapore's hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. The dish features bite-sized pieces of chicken marinated and wok-fried with dried chili, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, vinegar, and a cornstarch-thickened sauce, finished with sesame oil and spring onion. The name 'Kung Fu' evokes the precision and skill of wok cooking at high heat. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly tangy from vinegar, mildly spicy from dried chili, and aromatic from ginger and garlic. The texture is tender chicken with a lightly glossy sauce coating. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 129 kcal, 9.76 g protein, 6.98 g fat, 6.87 g carbohydrate, 3.03 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 402 mg sodium. The dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate and sugar content and the glycaemic-moderating effect of vinegar. However, the sodium content of 402 mg/100g raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free. It is typically served as part of a shared Chinese meal with steamed rice and vegetables.
common_user_queries: Is Kung Fu Chicken suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010375) with provenance marked as canonical and confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Kung Fu Chicken preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010375) as of 2026-04-16. The input record cultural.primary_cuisine was listed as 'Middle Eastern' which is inconsistent with the dish name, ingredients, and HPB classification; this has been corrected to 'Chinese-Singaporean' based on the dish's actual culinary origin and HPB food group classification. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on stir-fried chicken dishes with soy-based sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kung Fu Chicken was found in approved sources.
